title: Pricing Two Items
description: Use two clues to find two unknown prices.

miniLesson.title: Two Unknowns, Two Clues
miniLesson.paragraph: We introduced the concept of **system of equations** earlier. For this lesson, you can think of a system of equations as two clues about two unknowns. Each clue is really an equation: give each price a letter and write what you know. With $a$ for the apple and $b$ for the banana, the two clues become $a = 2$ and $a + b = 5$ - the same systems you solved with $x$ and $y$, just with letters that stand for prices. When one price is already known, **substitute** it into the other clue and solve for what is left. Always check your prices in **both** clues before you finish.

example.prompt: **1 apple costs $2.** Together, **1 apple and 1 banana** cost **$5**. Find the price of each.
example.explanation: As equations: $a = 2$ and $a + b = 5$. The apple is **$2**, so the banana is **$5** - **$2** = **$3**.

miniLesson.title: Substitution
miniLesson.paragraph: When one clue tells you a single item's price, **substitute** that value into the other clue. Replace the known item with its price, then the remaining math has just **one unknown** left to solve. With $p$ for the pen and $n$ for the notebook, the clues become $p = 1$ and $2p + n = 5$; substituting gives $2(1) + n = 5$, exactly the substitution step from systems of equations.

example.prompt: **1 pen costs $1.** **2 pens and 1 notebook** cost **$5**. Find the price of each.
example.explanation: As equations: $p = 1$ and $2p + n = 5$. Substitute $p = 1$: two pens cost **$2**, so the notebook is **$5** - **$2** = **$3**.

miniLesson.title: Elimination
miniLesson.paragraph: When both clues mix the two items, line them up and **subtract** one from the other. If an item appears the same number of times in both clues, subtracting **cancels** it, leaving a single price you can solve for. Writing $a$ for the apple and $o$ for the orange, the clues become $2a + o = 4$ and $a + o = 3$; subtracting them cancels $o$, the same elimination step from systems of equations.

example.prompt: **2 apples and 1 orange** cost **$4**. **1 apple and 1 orange** cost **$3**. Find the price of each.
example.explanation: As equations: $2a + o = 4$ and $a + o = 3$. Subtracting the clues cancels the orange: 1 apple = **$4** - **$3** = **$1**, so the orange is **$3** - **$1** = **$2**.

miniLesson.title: Scaling Before You Subtract
miniLesson.paragraph: Sometimes no item appears the **same number of times** in either clue, so subtracting right away cancels nothing. First **multiply** one whole clue so that an item lines up, then subtract to eliminate it. Scaling a clue means multiplying every part of it by the same number. Writing $b$ for the burger and $f$ for the fries, the clues become $2b + f = 13$ and $b + 2f = 11$; doubling the second clue gives $2b + 4f = 22$, so subtracting now cancels the burgers - the same scale-then-eliminate move you used on systems of equations.

example.prompt: **2 burgers and 1 fries** cost **$13**. **1 burger and 2 fries** cost **$11**. Find the price of each.
example.explanation: As equations: $2b + f = 13$ and $b + 2f = 11$. Neither item lines up, so double the second clue to $2b + 4f = 22$. Subtracting the first cancels the burgers: $3f$ = **$22** - **$13** = **$9**, so the fries are **$3**. Then $2b$ = **$13** - **$3** = **$10**, so a burger is **$5**.

miniLesson.title: Putting It Together
miniLesson.paragraph: Now pick the best tool for each problem: **substitute** when one price is given, and **eliminate** when both clues mix the items. This is the same choice you make on any system of equations - with $c$ for the coffee and $d$ for the donut, the clues become $2c + d = 8$ and $c + d = 5$. Whatever you choose, finish by checking both prices satisfy **both** clues.

example.prompt: **2 coffees and 1 donut** cost **$8**. **1 coffee and 1 donut** cost **$5**. Find the price of each.
example.explanation: As equations: $2c + d = 8$ and $c + d = 5$. Subtracting cancels the donut: 1 coffee = **$8** - **$5** = **$3**, so the donut is **$5** - **$3** = **$2**.
